II, 40.</bibl></cit> MVSA uoster, qui cum uulnus nouercae descripsisset adiecit: at, hercules, pater meus tanquam paries perfossus est. MVRREDIVS: patrocinium putat esse causae suae quod sanguinem misit. NEPOS LICINIVS ait: non est istud uulnus, sed ludentis adulteri
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote><lb/>cinis ipse sepulti <lb/>In genus hoc pugnat.</quote> <bibl>Ouid. Net. 13.503 sqq.</bibl></cit> poterat hoc contentus esse; adiecit:
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote xml:lang="grc"><lb/>ti/s nu/ moi h(me/rh h(/de, qeoi\ fi/loi; h)= ma/la xi/rw</quote> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 24.514">Hom. Od. 24.514.</bibl></cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote xml:lang="grc"><lb/>pollo\n d' o(/ge patro\s a)mei/nwn.</quote> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 6.479">Hom. Il. 6.479.</bibl></cit> SCAVRVS hunc sensum aliter dixit:

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote rend="blockquote"> <l><foreign xml:lang="grc">a)/ndra moi e)/nnepe, Mou=sa,</foreign> <note><hi rend="italic">Odyss. i. 1.</hi></note> </l></quote></cit> and

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote rend="blockquote"> <l><foreign xml:lang="grc">e)/spete nu=n moi, Mou=sai,</foreign> <note><hi rend="italic">Iliad</hi> ii. 484, etc.</note> </l></quote></cit>

bibls: <bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" n="Luc. 6.141">VI.141 sqq.</bibl>), pariter quondam in castello, atque ipse, obsessi.
<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0">Shelley, ' Prometheus Unbound,' Act iii., Scene i.</bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" n="Hdt. 2.4">Herodotus, ii., 4</bibl>.) Eudoxus seems to have dealt with this year and to have corrected it. He is probably alluded to by Virgil, 'Eclogue' iii., 41.

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" n="Hdt. 3.17">Herodotus, III., 17</bibl>. These Ethiopian races were supposed to live to the age of 120 years, drinking milk, and eating boiled flesh. On Cambyses's march his starving troops cast lots by tens for the one man who was to be eaten.
cits: <cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote xml:lang="eng"> <l>The rugged Charon fainted,</l> <l>And asked a navy, rather than a boat,</l> <l>To ferry over the sad world that came.</l> </quote> <bibl>(Ben Jonson, 'Catiline,' Act i., scene 1.)</bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote xml:lang="eng"> <l>'Thus far hath one of steep <placeName key="tgn,7011022">Parnassus</placeName>' brows</l> <l>Sufficed me: henceforth there is need of both,</l> <l>For my remaining enterprise.'</l> </quote> <bibl>Dante, 'Paradise,' i., 16 (Cary.)</bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote xml:lang="eng"> <l>When the boisterous sea,</l> <l>Without a breath of wind, hath knocked the sky.</l> </quote> <bibl>Ben Jonson's 'Masque of Queens.'</bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote xml:lang="eng"> <l>I have been all day, looking after</l> <l>A raven feeding on a quarter,</l> <l>And soon as she turned her beak to the south</l> <l>I snatched this morsel out of her mouth.'</l> </quote> <bibl>Ben Jonson.</bibl> </cit> But more probably the meaning is that the wolf's bite gave the flesh magical efficacy.

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote xml:lang="eng"> <l>It is, methinks, a morning full of fate!</l> <l>It riseth slowly, as her sullen car</l> <l>Had all the weight of sleep and death hung at it!</l> <l>And her sick head is bound about with clouds</l> <l>As if she threatened night ere noon of day.</l> </quote> <bibl>Ben Jonson, 'Catiline,' i., 1.</bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote xml:lang="eng">"Hath Jove no thunder?"</quote> <bibl>Ben Jonson, 'Catiline,' iii., 2.</bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote xml:lang="eng"> <l>All my being,</l> <l>Like him whom the Numidian Seps did thaw</l> <l>Into a dew with poison, is dissolved,</l> <l>Sinking through its foundations.</l> </quote> <bibl>Shelley, ' Prometheus Unbound,' Act iii., Scene i.</bibl> </cit>

bibls: <bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> Enn. 174 <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> (with <hi rend="italic">oras</hi> for <hi rend="italic">causas</hi> ).The question ( <hi rend="italic">numquid,</hi> etc.) is treated by Cicero as meaning <quote>Can you quote anything from the sixth book of the Annals?</quote> <hi rend="italic">ingentis</hi> is ace. plural. </note> </bibl>
<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> The author, presumably a tragic poet, is unknown. <hi rend="italic">Lartis= Luertius,</hi> son of Laertes. </bibl>
<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false">Probably from a lost comedy.</bibl>
<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Ter. Andr.</hi> I. i. 41. </bibl>
<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> From Furius, an old epic poet of the second century (not Furius Bibaculus), <hi rend="italic">cp.</hi> Hor. S. ii. v. 11. </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> xi. 142. A false explanation of the historic infinitive as involving the omission of some such word as <hi rend="italic">coeperunt.</hi> </bibl>
<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> Hor. <hi rend="italic">Od.</hi> I. iv. 13. </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> iv. 495. This third example does not correspond with the twofold division given by <hi rend="italic">utroque</hi> and may be spurious. </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> II. xv. It is an abuse to say <hi rend="italic">aedficant,</hi> which means literally "they make a house. </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> iv. 425. Dido is urging Anna to approach Aeneas and induce Aeneas to postpone his departure. Dido is no enemy from whom he need fly. </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> x. 92. Juno ironically pretends to have brought about the rape of Helen, which was in reality the work of Venus. </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> iv,. 379. Dido mocks the excuse of Aeneas that he had received the direct command of heaven to leave Carthage. </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Ecl.</hi> iv. 62. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> Those that have never smiled on their parents, neither does any god honour him by admitting him to his feats nor goddess deem him worthy of her bed." Although there can be no doubt as to the correctness of Politian's emendation in the passage as quoted here, it is against all MSS. authority, both of Virgil and Quintilian, and it is still frequently held that Virgil wrote <hi rend="italic">cui.</hi> </note> </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Pers.</hi> i. 9. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>I look at our dreary way of living.</quote> </note> </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> v. 248. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>He gives him a great talent-weight to carry.</quote> </note> </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> Hor. <hi rend="italic">Ep.</hi> I. i. 41. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>'Tis a virtue to shun vice.</quote> </note> </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> xi. 400. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> The figure consists in the use of <hi rend="italic">vel cum</hi> to introduce an independent sentence. <quote>Even when he claims, to tremble at my taunts.</quote> </note> </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> <hi rend="italic">An.</hi> i. 19. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote rend="blockquote"> <l part="N">But she had heard that even now a race</l> <l part="N">Was springing from the blood of fallen Troy.</l> </quote> Quintilian refers to the archaic <hi rend="italic">sed enim.</hi> </note> </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Georg.</hi> iv. 445. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>For who bade thee, of youths most bold.</quote> </note> The figure consists in the opening of a speech with <hi rend="italic">nam,</hi> or perhaps rather in saying <hi rend="italic">nam quis</hi> for <hi rend="italic">quisnam.</hi> </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> Cat. lxii. 45. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>While she remains unwed, so long is she dear to her own.</quote> Such is Quintilian's interpretation. The line, however, runs <hi rend="italic">sic virgo, dum intacta</hi> (MSS. of Catullus), <hi rend="italic">etc.,</hi> and is most naturally interpreted: <quote> Even so ( <hi rend="italic">i.e.</hi> like to a perfect blossom) is the maiden, while she remains unblemished and dear to her own. </quote> </note> </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Sat.</hi> II. vi. 83. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>Nor grudged him vetches nor the long-eared oat.</quote> </note> The gen. of respect is regarded as a Graecism. </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Ecl.</hi> x. 11: <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>For neither did Parnassus slope, nor yet/ The slopes of Pindus make delay for you.</quote> </note> </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> Ter. <hi rend="italic">Eun.</hi> I. ii. 5. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>Draw near the fire and you shall be more than warm enough.</quote> </note> </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> vii. 759 <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote rend="blockquote"> <l part="N">Thee did Angitia's grove bewail,</l> <l part="N">Thee too the glassy waves o' the Fucine lake.</l> </quote> The correspondence is to be found in <hi rend="italic">te</hi> (coming first in one and second in the other clause). </note> </bibl>
<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> i. 26. The translation is Watson's.</bibl>
<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> iii. 234; <hi rend="italic">participio</hi> = gerundive ( <hi rend="italic">gerendum</hi> ). </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> i. 399. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>Your ships and the flower of your young warriors.</quote> </note> </bibl>
<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> See XI. i. 24. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>O happy Rome, born in my consulship.</quote> </note> </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> x. 1. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>Meanwhile Olympus' halls omnipotent / Are wide unbarred.</quote> </note> </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Ecl.</hi> ix. 45. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>I have the numbers, could I but find the words.</quote> In this case the nearest translation of <hi rend="italic">numeri</hi> would be <quote>tune.</quote> But, strictly speaking, it refers to the rhythm of the tune. </note> </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Odes.</hi> IV. ii. 11. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>And sweeps along in numbers free from laws.</quote> </note> </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> From an unknown tragedian. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>Lo, I am lord at Argos, where to me I Pelops the sceptre left.</quote> </note> </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Ter. Eun.</hi> I. i. 1. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>What shall I do then? Not go even now?</quote> </note> The <hi rend="italic">pyrrhic</hi> never forms a separate foot, but does form part of the <hi rend="italic">anapaest, tribrach</hi> and <hi rend="italic">dactyl</hi> and it is in this connexion that it is mentioned by Quintilian. </bibl>

<bibl xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" default="false"> Cat. xxix. 1. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>Who save a lecherous gambling glutton can endure to gaze on such a sight as this</quote> </note> </bibl>
cits: <cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">So strong is custom formed in early years.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false">Georg. ii. 272.</bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">panditur interea domus omnipotentis Olympi</l> </quote> <bibl default="false">Aen. x. 1</bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">totis usque adeo turbatur agris.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false">Ecl. i. 11</bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Once supply the thought,</l> <l part="N">And words will follow swift as soon as sought</l> </quote> <bibl default="false">Ars Poetica, 311.</bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">quae circus litora, circum piscosos scopulos,</l> </quote> <bibl default="false">Aen. iv. 254.</bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">The wandering moon and labours of the Sun</l> </quote> <bibl default="false">Aen. i. 742.</bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">This I enjoin and urge and urge anew</l> </quote> <bibl default="false">Verg. Aen. iii. 436.</bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">For wind turns Phoebe's face to ruddy gold</l> </quote> <bibl default="false">Verg. G. i. 431.</bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="F">The crow</l> <l part="N">With full voice, good-for-naught, invites the rain.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">ib.</hi> i. 388. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">A shrewd retort! Could it be otherwise?</l> <l part="I">A foolish question makes for smart replies.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false">Origin unknown.</bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">There lie the task and toil!</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> vi. 128. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Sudden her fingers let the shuttle fall</l> <l part="I">And all the thread was spilled,</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> ix. 474. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">In his smooth breast the gaping wound</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">ib.</hi> xi. 40. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">In all her body not a grain of salt!</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Cat.</hi> lxxxvi. 4. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Who may the causes vast of war unfold?</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> Enn. 174 <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> (with <hi rend="italic">oras</hi> for <hi rend="italic">causas</hi> ).The question ( <hi rend="italic">numquid,</hi> etc.) is treated by Cicero as meaning <quote>Can you quote anything from the sixth book of the Annals?</quote> <hi rend="italic">ingentis</hi> is ace. plural. </note> </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Had not Ulysses Lartius intervened.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> The author, presumably a tragic poet, is unknown. <hi rend="italic">Lartis= Luertius,</hi> son of Laertes. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">What men call wisdom is a legacy,</l> </quote> <bibl default="false">Probably from a lost comedy.</bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="F">Chill shudderings shake my limbs</l> <l part="N">And all my blood is curdled cold with fear;</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> iii. 29 </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="F">And trembling mothers clasped</l> <l part="N">Their children to their breast.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> vii. 518. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Thence like fierce wolves beneath the cloud of night,</l> </quote> <bibl default="false">Aen. ii. 355.</bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="M">Like the bird that flies</l> <l part="N">Around the shore and the fish-haunted reef,</l> <l part="N">Skimming the deep.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false">Aen. iv. 254.</bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">As when Apollo wintry Lycia leaves,</l> <l part="N">And Xanthus' streams, or visits Delos' isle,</l> <l part="N">His mother's home,</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> iv. 143. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N"> Thence like fierce wolves beneath the cloud of <lb/> night, </l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> ii. 355. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">As when, their barriers down, the chariots speed</l> <l part="N">Lap after lap; in vain the charioteer</l> <l part="N">Tightens the curb: his steeds ungovernable</l> <l part="N">Sweep him away nor heeds the car the rein.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Georg. i.</hi> 512. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Complaisance wins us friends, truth enmity.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Ter. Andr.</hi> I. i. 41. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Death is not bitter, but the approach to death.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false">Author unknown.</bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="F">The miser lacks</l> <l part="N"> That which he has no less than what he has <lb/> not. </l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> Publil. <hi rend="italic">Syr. Sent.</hi> 486. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Is it so bitter, then, to die?</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> xii. 646. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Such toil it was to found the Roman race!</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> i. 33. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">And gave his fleet the rein,</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> vi. 1. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Did the Argive bulwark fall by sword or fate?</l> </quote> <bibl default="false">From an unknown tragedian.</bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">The shepherd sits unknowing on the height</l> <l part="N"> Listening the roar from some far mountain <lb/> brow. </l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> ii. 307. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Araxes' flood that scorns a bridge,</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> viii. 728. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">And with venom arm the steel.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> ix. 773. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Jove with white snow the wintry Alps bespewed.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> From Furius, an old epic poet of the second century (not Furius Bibaculus), <hi rend="italic">cp.</hi> Hor. S. ii. v. 11. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">The Arcadians to the gates began to rush;</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> xi. 142. A false explanation of the historic infinitive as involving the omission of some such word as <hi rend="italic">coeperunt.</hi> </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Behold, the steers</l> <l part="N"> Bring back the plough suspended from the <lb/> yoke, </l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Ed.</hi> ii. 61 </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Neptune admitted to the land</l> <l part="N">Protects the fleets from blasts of Aquilo.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">A. P.</hi> 63. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Ucalegon burns next.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> ii. 311. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Pale death with equal foot knocks at the poor man's door</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> Hor. <hi rend="italic">Od.</hi> I. iv. 13. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">There pale diseases dwell and sad old age;</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> vi. 275 </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Father of gods and king of men,</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> i. 65. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">The arms which he, the traitor, left</l> <l part="N">Fixed on the chamber wall.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> iv. 495. This third example does not correspond with the twofold division given by <hi rend="italic">utroque</hi> and may be spurious. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">A horse they build by Pallas' art divine,</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> II. xv. It is an abuse to say <hi rend="italic">aedficant,</hi> which means literally "they make a house. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">O ship, new waves will bear thee back to sea.</l> <l part="N">What dost thou? Make the haven, come what may,</l> </quote> <bibl default="false">Hor. Od. i. xiv. 1.</bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">But now</l> <l part="N">A mighty length of plain we have travelled o'er;</l> <l part="N">'Tis time to loose our horses' steaming necks.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false">Georg. II. 541.</bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="F">Now was the time</l> <l part="N">When the first sleep to weary mortals comes</l> <l part="N">Stealing its way, the sweetest boon of heaven.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> ii. 268. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">win rocks that threaten heaven.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> i. 162. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Thou wouldst have deemed</l> <l part="N">That Cyclad isles uprooted swam the deep.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> viii. 691. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Swifter than the levin's wings;</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> v. 319. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">She would fly</l> <l part="N">Even o'er the tops of the unsickled corn,</l> <l part="N">Nor as she ran would bruise the tender ears.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> vii. 808. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Are any left</l> <l part="N">That still adore Juno's divinity?</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> i. 48. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">To what dost thou not drive the hearts of men,</l> <l part="N">Accursed greed of gold?</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> iii. 56. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Will they not rush to arms and follow forth</l> <l part="N">From all the city?</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> iv. 592. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Gape now, wide earth.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false">Unknown.</bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">I swore not with the Greeks</l> <l part="N">At Aulis to uproot the race of Troy.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> iv. 425. Dido is urging Anna to approach Aeneas and induce Aeneas to postpone his departure. Dido is no enemy from whom he need fly. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">'Twas I that led the Dardan gallant on</l> <l part="N">To storm the bridal bed of Sparta's queen!</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> x. 92. Juno ironically pretends to have brought about the rape of Helen, which was in reality the work of Venus. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Fit task, I ween, for gods!</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> iv,. 379. Dido mocks the excuse of Aeneas that he had received the direct command of heaven to leave Carthage. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="F">Might I not have lived,</l> <l part="N">From wedlock free, a life without a stain,</l> <l part="N">Happy as beasts are happy?</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> iv. 550. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">O mother, happy in thy spouse!</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Met.</hi> x. 422. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">qui non risere parentes</l> <l part="N">nec deus hunc mensa dea nec dignata cubili est,</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Ecl.</hi> iv. 62. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> Those that have never smiled on their parents, neither does any god honour him by admitting him to his feats nor goddess deem him worthy of her bed." Although there can be no doubt as to the correctness of Politian's emendation in the passage as quoted here, it is against all MSS. authority, both of Virgil and Quintilian, and it is still frequently held that Virgil wrote <hi rend="italic">cui.</hi> </note> </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">nostrum istud vivere triste aspxei,</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Pers.</hi> i. 9. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>I look at our dreary way of living.</quote> </note> </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">magnum dat ferre talentum,</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> v. 248. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>He gives him a great talent-weight to carry.</quote> </note> </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">virtus est vitium fugere,</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> Hor. <hi rend="italic">Ep.</hi> I. i. 41. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>'Tis a virtue to shun vice.</quote> </note> </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">vel cum se pavidum contra mea iurgia iactat</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> xi. 400. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> The figure consists in the use of <hi rend="italic">vel cum</hi> to introduce an independent sentence. <quote>Even when he claims, to tremble at my taunts.</quote> </note> </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">progeniem sed enim Troiano a sanguine duci</l> <l part="N">audierat.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">An.</hi> i. 19. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote rend="blockquote"> <l part="N">But she had heard that even now a race</l> <l part="N">Was springing from the blood of fallen Troy.</l> </quote> Quintilian refers to the archaic <hi rend="italic">sed enim.</hi> </note> </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">nam quis te iuvenum confidentissime,</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Georg.</hi> iv. 445. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>For who bade thee, of youths most bold.</quote> </note> The figure consists in the opening of a speech with <hi rend="italic">nam,</hi> or perhaps rather in saying <hi rend="italic">nam quis</hi> for <hi rend="italic">quisnam.</hi> </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">tam magis illa tremens et tristibus etffra flammis,</l> <l part="N">quam magis effuso crudescunt sanguine pugnae.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> vii. 787. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">dum innupta manet, dum cara suis est,</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> Cat. lxii. 45. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>While she remains unwed, so long is she dear to her own.</quote> Such is Quintilian's interpretation. The line, however, runs <hi rend="italic">sic virgo, dum intacta</hi> (MSS. of Catullus), <hi rend="italic">etc.,</hi> and is most naturally interpreted: <quote> Even so ( <hi rend="italic">i.e.</hi> like to a perfect blossom) is the maiden, while she remains unblemished and dear to her own. </quote> </note> </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">nec ciceris net longae invidit avenae.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Sat.</hi> II. vi. 83. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>Nor grudged him vetches nor the long-eared oat.</quote> </note> The gen. of respect is regarded as a Graecism. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">nam neque Parnasi vobis iuga, nam neque Pindi,</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Ecl.</hi> x. 11: <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>For neither did Parnassus slope, nor yet/ The slopes of Pindus make delay for you.</quote> </note> </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">accede ad ignen, iam calesces plus satis;</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> Ter. <hi rend="italic">Eun.</hi> I. ii. 5. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>Draw near the fire and you shall be more than warm enough.</quote> </note> </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Nor let thy vineyards slope toward the west,</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Georg.</hi> ii. 298. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="F">In that hour</l> <l part="N">Be it not mine beneath the open sky</l> <l part="N">To court soft sleep nor on the forest ridge</l> <l part="N">Amid the grass to lie.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Georg.</hi> iii. 435. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="F">The Decii too,</l> <l part="N">The Marii and Camilli, names of might,</l> <l part="N">The Scipios, stubborn warriors, aye, and thee,</l> <l part="N">Great Caesar.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Georg.</hi> ii. 169. (Rhoades' translation). </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">What do I say? Where am I?</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> iv. 595. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Ah! Corydon, Corydon.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Ecl.</hi> ii. 69. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">te nemus Angitiae, vitrea te Fucinus unda.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> vii. 759 <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote rend="blockquote"> <l part="N">Thee did Angitia's grove bewail,</l> <l part="N">Thee too the glassy waves o' the Fucine lake.</l> </quote> The correspondence is to be found in <hi rend="italic">te</hi> (coming first in one and second in the other clause). </note> </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Iphitus too with me and Pelias came,</l> <l part="N">Iphitus bowed with age and Pelias</l> <l part="N">Slow-limping with the wound Ulysses gave.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> ii. 435. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N"> Here corn, there grapes, elsewhere the growth <lb/> of trees </l> <l part="N">More freely rises,</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Georg.</hi> i. 54. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="F">Is then to know in thee</l> <l part="N">Nothing unless another know thou knowest?</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> i. 26. The translation is Watson's.</bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">And ye,</l> <l part="N">Pierian Muses, shall enhance their worth</l> <l part="N">For Gallus; Gallus, he for whom each hour</l> <l part="N">My love burns stronger.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Cat.</hi> I. i. 2. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Myself before my very eyes I saw:</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> xii. 638. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">His house and home and arms</l> <l part="N">And Amyclean hound and Cretan quiver;</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Georg.</hi> iii. 344. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N"> You—while the goats looked goatish-we know <lb/> who, </l> <l part="N"> And in what chapel—(but the kind Nymphs <lb/> laughed). </l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Ecl.</hi> iii. 8. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Sociis tunc arma capessant</l> <l part="N">Edico et dira bellum cum gene gerendumn.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> iii. 234; <hi rend="italic">participio</hi> = gerundive ( <hi rend="italic">gerendum</hi> ). </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">The miser lacks</l> <l part="N">That which he has no less than what he has not.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Syrus</hi> 486 (Ribbeck). </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">I labour to be brief, I turn obscure,</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> Hor <hi rend="italic">A.P.</hi> 25. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">vuppesque tuae pubesque tuorum,</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> i. 399. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>Your ships and the flower of your young warriors.</quote> </note> </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Ofortunatam natam me consule Romam.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> See XI. i. 24. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>O happy Rome, born in my consulship.</quote> </note> </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Panditur snterea domus omnipotentis Olympi</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> x. 1. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>Meanwhile Olympus' halls omnipotent / Are wide unbarred.</quote> </note> </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Numeros memini, si verba tenerem</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Ecl.</hi> ix. 45. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>I have the numbers, could I but find the words.</quote> In this case the nearest translation of <hi rend="italic">numeri</hi> would be <quote>tune.</quote> But, strictly speaking, it refers to the rhythm of the tune. </note> </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Numerisquefertur</l> <l part="N">Lege solutis.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Odes.</hi> IV. ii. 11. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>And sweeps along in numbers free from laws.</quote> </note> </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">En, impero Argis, sceptra mi liquit Pelops.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> From an unknown tragedian. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>Lo, I am lord at Argos, where to me I Pelops the sceptre left.</quote> </note> </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">quid igiturfaciam? non earn, ne nunc quidem?</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Ter. Eun.</hi> I. i. 1. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>What shall I do then? Not go even now?</quote> </note> The <hi rend="italic">pyrrhic</hi> never forms a separate foot, but does form part of the <hi rend="italic">anapaest, tribrach</hi> and <hi rend="italic">dactyl</hi> and it is in this connexion that it is mentioned by Quintilian. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Quis hoc potest videre, quis potest pati,</l> <l part="N">nisi impudicus et vorax et aleo?</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> Cat. xxix. 1. <note anchored="true" place="unspecified"> <quote>Who save a lecherous gambling glutton can endure to gaze on such a sight as this</quote> </note> </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">The ivy creeps amid your victor bays</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Ecl.</hi> viii. 13. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Ye gods, such dread calamity avert!</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> iii. 620. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Not for me</l> <l part="N">To claim such honour!</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> i. 335. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Thou this poor kingdom dost on me bestow.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> i. 78. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Thou vanquish him in song?</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Ecl.</hi> iii. 25. </bibl> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Art thou, then, that Aeneas?</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen</hi> i. 617. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="F">And of fear,</l> <l part="N">Do thou accuse me, Drances!</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> xi. 383. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <quote> <l part="N">Nothing before and nothing behind but the sky and the Ocean.</l> </quote> <bibl default="false"> <hi rend="italic">Aen.</hi> iii. 193. </bibl> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"A person may be called <foreign xml:lang="lat">clarus</foreign> either on account of his great actions and merits; or on account of some honor which he has obtained, as the consuls were called <foreign xml:lang="lat">clarissimi viri;</foreign> or on account of great expectations which are formed from him. But since the worth of him who is <foreign xml:lang="lat">clarus</foreign> is known by all, it appears that the mind is here called <foreign xml:lang="lat">clarus</foreign> because its nature is such that pre-eminence is generally attributed to it, and the attention of all directed toward it."</quote><bibl>Dietsch.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"<foreign xml:lang="lat">Negotia</foreign> is a common word with Sallust, for which other writers would use <foreign xml:lang="lat">res, facta.</foreign>"</quote><bibl>Gerlach.</bibl></cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote><foreign xml:lang="lat">"Cujus rei nos ipsi sumus auctores, ejus culpam rebus externis attribuimus."</foreign></quote><bibl>Müller.</bibl></cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"All things are in its power."</quote><bibl>Dietsch.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"<foreign xml:lang="lat">Sub ditione tenet.</foreign> So <placeName key="tgn,1125260">Jupiter</placeName>, Ov. Met. i. 197: <foreign xml:lang="lat">Quum mihi qui fulmen, qui vos habeoque rogoque.</foreign>"</quote><bibl>Bernouf.</bibl></cit> So Aristippus said,
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote><foreign xml:lang="lat">"Illo vocabulo civilia, hoc militaria munera, significantur."</foreign></quote><bibl>Dietsch.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"<foreign xml:lang="lat">Portendere</foreign> in a pregnant sense, meaning not merely to indicate, but <foreign xml:lang="lat">quasi secum ferre,</foreign> to carry along with them."</quote><bibl>Kritzius.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"Sallust obtained the quæstorship a few years after the conspiracy of Catiline, about the time when the state was agitated by the disorders of Clodius and his party. He was tribune of the people, A.U.C. 701, the year in which Clodius was killed by <placeName key="tgn,1013243">Milo</placeName>. He was prætor in 708, when Cæsar had made himself ruler. In the expression <foreign xml:lang="lat">quales viri</foreign>, etc., he alludes chiefly to Cato, who, when he stood for the prætorship, was unsuccessful."</quote><bibl>Bernouf.</bibl></cit> Kritzius defends
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"Cæsar chose the worthy and unworthy, as suited his own purposes, to be members of the senate."</quote><bibl>Bernouf.</bibl></cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"All things, both divine and human, were so changed, that their previous condition was entirely subverted."</quote><bibl>Dietsch.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"I know not why interpreters should find any difficulty in this passage. I understand it to signify simply since the Romans became so great as they were in the time of <placeName key="tgn,2069580">Hannibal</placeName>; for, before that period they had suffered even heavier calamities, especially from the Gauls."</quote><bibl>Cortius.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"He was King of the Masæsyli in <placeName key="tgn,7016752">Numidia</placeName>; was at first an enemy to the Carthaginians (Liv. xxiv. 48), and afterward their friend (Liv. xxviii. 17). He then changed sides again, and made a treaty with Scipio ; but having at length been offered the hand of Sophonisba, the daughter of Asdrubal, in marriage, he accepted it, and returned into alliance with the Carthaginians. Being subsequently taken prisoner by Masinissa and Lælius, the lieutenant of Scipio, (Liv. xxx. 2) he was carried into <placeName key="tgn,1000080">Italy</placeName>, and died at <placeName key="perseus,Tibur">Tibur</placeName> (Liv. xxx. 45)."</quote><bibl>Bernouf.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"<foreign xml:lang="lat">Universæ potius civitatis, quám privatorum gratiam quærendo.</foreign>"</quote><bibl>Bernouf.</bibl></cit> The words can only be rendered periphrastically.
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"<foreign xml:lang="lat">Per fidem quam tu rex (futurus) mihi regi præstare debes.</foreign>"</quote><bibl>Burnouf.</bibl></cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote xml:lang="lat">"Per fidem quæ decet in regno, i.e. regem."</quote><bibl>Dietsch.</bibl></cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"<foreign xml:lang="lat">Per eam fidem, quâ esse decet eum qui regnum obtinet.</foreign>"</quote><bibl>Kritzius.</bibl></cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"<foreign xml:lang="grc">Νόμιζ' ἀδελφοὺσ τοὺσ ἀληθινοὺσ φίλους.</foreign> Menander."</quote><bibl>Wasse.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"The <foreign xml:lang="lat">proximus lictor</foreign> was he who, when the lictors walked before the prince or magistrate in a regular line, one behind the other, was last, or next to the person on whom they attended."</quote><bibl>Cortius.</bibl></cit> He would thus be ready to receive the great man's commands, and be in immediate communication with him. We must suppose either that Sallust merely speaks in conformity with the practice of the Romans, or, what is more probable, that the Roman custom of being preceded by lictors had been adopted in

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"The word province, in this place, signifies that part of <placeName key="tgn,7001242">Africa</placeName> which, after the destruction of <placeName key="perseus,Carthage">Carthage</placeName>, fell to the Romans by the right of conquest, in opposition to the kingdom of Micipsa."</quote><bibl>Wasse.</bibl></cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"The embassadors of Jugurtha, and Adherbal in person, are admitted into the senate-house to plead their cause."</quote><bibl>Bernouf.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote xml:lang="lat">"Priscianus, lib. xiii., de præpositione agens Secundum, inquit, quando pro <foreign xml:lang="grc">κατὰ</foreign> et <foreign xml:lang="grc">μετὰ</foreign> accipitur, loco prœpositionis est. Sallustius in Jugurthino: secundum ea, uti deditis uterer. —Videlicet hoc dicit, Secundum in Sallustii exemplo, post vel proximè significare."</quote><bibl>Rivius.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"<foreign xml:lang="lat">In manu, fuit</foreign> is simply <foreign xml:lang="lat">in potestate fuit.</foreign>—Ter. Hec., iv. 4, 44: <foreign xml:lang="lat">Uxor quid faciat in manu non est meâ.</foreign>"</quote><bibl>Cortius.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote><foreign xml:lang="lat">"Si omnia mihi incolumia manerent, neque quidquam rerum mearum (s. præsidiorum) amisissem, neque Jugurtha aliique mihi ex necessariis inimici facti essent."</foreign></quote><bibl>Kritzius.</bibl></cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote><foreign xml:lang="lat">"Paullatim interire."</foreign></quote><bibl>Cortius.</bibl></cit> Lucret. ii.

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"A happy expression; since the effect of old age on the bodily frame is not to break it in pieces suddenly, but to dissolve it, as it were, gradually and imperceptibly."</quote><bibl>Bernouf.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"This is not the prince that was murdered by Jugurtha, but the king who succeeded him; he was grandson of Masinissa, son of Gulussa, and father of <placeName key="tgn,1094266">Juba</placeName>. After <placeName key="tgn,1094266">Juba</placeName> was killed at <placeName key="tgn,6005912">Thapsus</placeName>, Cæsar reduced <placeName key="tgn,7016752">Numidia</placeName> to the condition of a province, and appointed Sallust over it, who had thus opportunities of gaining a knowledge of the country, and of consulting the books written in the language of it."</quote><bibl>Bernouf.</bibl></cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"See Pompon. Mel. i. 4; Plin. H. N. v. 4, 6, 8, v. 2, xxi. 13; Herod. iv. 159, 168."</quote><bibl>Gerlach</bibl></cit> The name

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"<foreign xml:lang="lat">Intra oceanum</foreign> is differently explained by different commentators. Cortius, Müller and Gerlach, understand the parts bounded by the ocean, lying close upon it, and stretching toward the west; while Langius thinks that the regions more remote from the <placeName key="tgn,7014206">Atlantic Ocean</placeName>, and extending toward the east, are meant. But Langius did not consider that those who had inverted keels of vessels for cottages, could not have strayed far from the ocean, but must have settled in parts bordering upon it. And this is what is signified by <foreign xml:lang="lat">intra oceanum.</foreign> For <foreign xml:lang="lat">intra aliquam rem</foreign> is not always used to denote what is actually in a thing, and circumscribed by its boundaries, but what approaches toward it, and reaches close to it."</quote><bibl>Kritzius.</bibl></cit> He then instances

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"It is not <placeName key="tgn,7001313">Hippo Regius</placeName>" (now called <foreign xml:lang="lat">Bona</foreign>) "that is meant, but another Hippo, otherwise called <foreign xml:lang="lat">Diarrhytum</foreign> or <foreign xml:lang="lat">Zarytum,</foreign> situate in Zengitana, not far from <placeName key="tgn,7018163">Utica</placeName>. This is shown by the order in which the places are named, as has already been observed by Cortius."</quote><bibl>Kritzius.</bibl></cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"<foreign xml:lang="lat">Catabathmon initium ponens Sallustius ab eo discedit.</foreign>"</quote><bibl>Kritzius.</bibl></cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"<foreign xml:lang="lat">Si quis secundum mare pergat.</foreign>"</quote><bibl>Wasse.</bibl></cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote><foreign xml:lang="lat">"Ultra Numidiam, meridiem versus."</foreign></quote><bibl>Burnouf.</bibl></cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"It fell below the truth, not telling the whole of the atrocity that had been committed."</quote><bibl>Gruter.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"Priscian (xviii. 26) interprets <foreign xml:lang="lat">clemens 'non nimius,'</foreign> alluding to this passage of Sallust."</quote><bibl>Kritzius.</bibl></cit> All the later commentators have adopted this interpretation, except Bernouf, who adopts the supposition of Ciacconius, that a vague and uncertain rumor is meant.
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"That is, the right of avenging himself."</quote><bibl>Rupertus.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"The great-grandson of him who was pronounced by the senate to be <foreign xml:lang="lat">vir optimus;</foreign> and son of him who, though holding no office at the time, took part in putting to death Tiberius Gracchus. He was "consul with Bestia, A.U.C. 643, and died in his consulship. Cic. Brut., 34."</quote><bibl>Bernouf.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"He had been on the side of the nobility against the Gracchi, and was therefore in favor with the senate. After his consulship he was accused and condemned by the Mamilian law (c. 40), for having received money from Jugurtha, Cic. Brut. c. 34. De Brosses thinks that he was the grandfather of that Bestia who was engaged in the conspiracy of Catiline."</quote><bibl>Bernouf.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"In order that Jugurtha might have confidence in Bestia, Sextius the quæstor was sent as a sort of hostage into one of Jugurtha's towns."</quote><bibl>Cortius.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"The commencement of it, there is little doubt, is imitated from Cato, of whose speech <foreign xml:lang="lat">De Lusitanis</foreign> the following fragment is extant in Aul. Gell. xiii. 24: <foreign xml:lang="lat">Multa me dehortata sunt huc prodire, anni, œtas, vox vires, senectus."</foreign></quote><bibl>Kritzius.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"It was at this time, A.U.C. 641, twenty-two years since the death of Tiberius Gracchus, and ten since that of Caius; Sallust, or Memmius, not to appear to make too nice a computation, takes a mean."</quote><bibl>Bernouf</bibl></cit> The manuscripts however, vary; some read fifteen, and others twelve. Cortius conjectured twenty, as a rounder number, which Kritzius and Dietsch have inserted in their texts. Twenty is also found in the Editio Victoriana, Florence,

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"They offended against the laws, when they took bribes from an enemy ; against the honor of Rome, when they did what was unworthy of it, and greatly to its injury; and against gods and men, against all divine and human obligations, when they granted to a wicked prince not only impunity, but even rewards, for his crimes."</quote><bibl>Dietsch.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"Those times, which immediately succeeded the deaths of the Gracchi, and which were distinguished for the tyranny of the nobles, and the humiliation of the people; these times, in which the people have begun to rouse their spirit and exert their liberty."</quote><bibl>Burnouf.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"No name was more hated at Rome than that of a king; and no sentiment, accordingly, could have been better adapted to inflame the minds of Memmius's hearers, than that which he here utters."</quote><bibl>Dietsch.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"Some foolishly interpret <foreign xml:lang="lat">auxilium</foreign> as signifying <foreign xml:lang="lat">auxilium tribunicium,</foreign> the aid of the tribunes; but it is evident to me that Sallust means aid against the injuries of bad men, i.e. revenge or punishment."</quote><bibl>Kritzius.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"If injuries are repressed, or prevented, there will be less need for the help of good men, and it will be of less consequence if they become inactive."</quote><bibl>Dietsch.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"In such a garb as accused persons, or suppliants, were accustomed to adopt, when they wished to excite compassion, putting on a mean dress, and allowing their hair and beard to grow."</quote><bibl>Burnouf.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"These words have given rise to wonderful hallucinations; for Quintilian, ix. 3. 17, having observed that many expressions of Sallust are borrowed from the Greek, as <foreign xml:lang="lat">Vulgus amat fieri,</foreign> all interpreters, from Cortius downward, have thought that the structure of Sallust's words must be Greek, and have taken <foreign xml:lang="lat">ira,</foreign> in this passage, for an ablative, and <foreign xml:lang="lat">quœ</foreign> for a nominative plural. Gerlach has even gone so far as to take liberties with the words cited by Quintilian, and to correct them, please the gods, into <foreign xml:lang="lat">quœ in vulgus amat fieri.</foreign> But how could there have been such want of penetration in learned critics, such deficiency in the knowledge of the two languages, that, when the imitation of the Greek, noticed by Quintilian, has reference merely to the word <foreign xml:lang="grc">φιλεῖ,</foreign> <foreign xml:lang="lat">amat,</foreign> they should think of extending it to the dependence of a singular verb on a neuter plural ? With truth, indeed, though with much simplicity, does Gerlach observe, that you will in vain seek for instances of this mode of expression in other writers."</quote><bibl>Kritzius.</bibl></cit> Dietsch agrees with Kritzius and there will, I hope, be no further doubt that

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"The right of voting was not extended to all the Latin people till A.U.C. 664, and the Italian allies did not obtain it till some years afterward."</quote><bibl>Kritzius.</bibl></cit> So that at this period, which was twenty years earlier, their influence could only be employed in an underhand way. Compare c. 42.

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"<foreign xml:lang="lat">Ducere</foreign> conveys the notion of cunning and fraud; <foreign xml:lang="lat">trahere</foreign> of some degree of force; <foreign xml:lang="lat">rapere</foreign> of open violence."</quote><bibl>Müller.</bibl></cit> The words chiefly refer to offices in the state, as is apparent from what follows.

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote xml:lang="lat"><l>Quid quod usque proximos</l> <l>Revellis agri terminos, et ultra</l> <l>Limites clientium</l> <l>Salis avarus? Pellitur paternos</l> <l>In sinu ferens deos</l> <l>Et uxor et vir, sordidosque natos.</l></quote> <bibl>Hor. Od., ii. 18.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote><l>What can this impious av'rice stay ?</l> <l>Their sacred landmarks torn away,</l> <l>You plunge into your neighbor's grounds,</l> <l>And overleap your client's bounds,</l> <l>Helpless the wife and husband flee</l> <l>And in their arms, expell'd by thee,</l> <l>Their household gods, adored in vain,</l> <l>Their infants, too, a sordid train.</l></quote> <bibl>Francis.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"That is, if the nobility had been truly worthy characters, they would rather have yielded to the Gracchi, than have revenged any wrong that they had received frem them in an unprincipled manner."</quote><bibl>Dietsch.</bibl></cit> Thus this is a reflection on the nobles; in which notion of the passage Allen concurs with Dietsch. Others, as Cortius, think it a reflection on the too great violence of the Gracchi. The brevity with which Sallust had expressed himself makes it difficult to decide. Kritzius, who thinks that the remark is in praise of the Gracchi, supplies the ellipse thus:

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"Other matters, unconnected with the war against Jugurtha, he thought that he would have to manage in conjunction with his colleague, and that, consequently, he might give but partial attention to them; but that the war in Numidia was committed to his sole care."</quote><bibl>Cortius.</bibl></cit> Other interpretations of these words have been suggested; but they are fanciful and unworthy of notice.

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"The words <foreign xml:lang="lat">sed neque muniebantur ea</foreign> are wanting in almost all the manuscripts, as well as in all the editions, except that of Cyprianus Popma."</quote><bibl>Kritzius.</bibl></cit> Gerlach, however had, previously to Kritz, inserted them in his text though in brackets; for he supposed them to be a mere conjecture of some scribe, who was not satisfied with a single

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"<foreign xml:lang="lat">Frumenti et omnium rerum quarum in bello usus est, largam copiam.</foreign>"</quote><bibl>Kritzius.</bibl></cit> I follow the text of Cortius (retaining the words

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote><foreign xml:lang="lat">"Periti loci et regionis"</foreign></quote><bibl>Cortius.</bibl></cit> Or it may mean knowing what they were to do, while the enemy would be

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"Through dread of the severest punishments if they should fall into the hands of the Romans. Valerius Maximus, ii. 7, speaks of deserters having been deprived of their hands by Quintus Fabius Maximus; of others who were crucified or beheaded by the elder Africanus; of others who were thrown to wild beasts by Africanus the younger; and of others who were sentenced by Paulus Æmilius to be trampled to death by elephants. Hence it appears that the punishment of deserters was left to the pleasure of the general."</quote><bibl>Bernouf.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"The words <foreign xml:lang="lat">quœ proxima est Numidiœ</foreign> Cortius would eject as superfluous and spurious. But it is to be understood that Metellus did not distribute his troops through the whole of the province, but in that part which is nearest to Numidia, in order that they might be easily assembled in case of an attack of the enemy or any other emergency. There is, therefore, no need to read with the Bipont edition and Müller, <foreign xml:lang="lat">quà proxima,</foreign> etc. though this is in itself not a bad conjecture."</quote><bibl>Kritzius.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"The town of Tisidium is nowhere else mentioned. Strabo (xvii. 3, p. 488, Ed. Tauch.) speaks of a place named <foreign xml:lang="grc">Τισιαιοὶ,</foreign> which was utterly destroyed, and not a vestige of it left."</quote><bibl>Gerlach</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"Marius was either a sincere believer in the absurd superstitions and dreams of the soothsayers, or pretended to be so, from a knowledge of the nature of mankind, who are eager to listen to wonders, and are more willing to be deceived than to be taught."</quote><bibl>Bernouf.</bibl></cit> See Plutarch, Life of Marius. He could interpret omens for himself, according to Valerius Maximus, i. 5.
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"Every one knows that Romans of equestrian dignity were accustomed to trade in the provinces."</quote><bibl>Burnouf.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"<foreign xml:lang="lat">Suffragatio</foreign> was the zealous recommendation of those who solicited the votes of their fellow-citizens in favor of some candidate. See Festus, s. v. <foreign xml:lang="lat">Suffragatores,</foreign> p. 266, Lindem."</quote><bibl>Dietsch.</bibl></cit> It was honorable, in the case of Marius, as it was without bribery, and seemed to have the good of the republic in view.
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote><foreign xml:lang="lat">"In primâ tectorum parte."</foreign></quote><bibl>Kritzius.</bibl></cit> The roofs were flat.

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"As he was a Latin, he was not protected by the Porcian law (see Cat., c. 51), though how far this law had power in the camp, is not agreed."</quote><bibl>Allen.</bibl></cit> Gerlach thinks that it had the same power in the camp as elsewhere, with reference to Roman citizens. But Roman citizenship was not extended to the Latins till the end of the Social War, A.U.C. 662. Plutarch, however, in his Life of Caius Gracchus (c. 9), speaks of Livius Drusus having been abetted by the patricians in proposing a law for exempting the Latin soldiers from being flogged, about thirty years earlier; and it seems to have been passed, but, from this passage of Sallust, appears not to have remained in force. Lipsius touches on this obscure point in his

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"By <foreign xml:lang="lat">extremâ Africa</foreign> Gerlach rightly understands the eastern part of Africa, bordering on Egypt, and at a great distance from Numidia."</quote><bibl>Kritzius.</bibl></cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"Understand Masinissa's, Micipsa's, or Jugurtha's."</quote><bibl>Bernouf.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote><foreign xml:lang="lat">"Nihil aliud est quàm vitam suam, </foreign> sc. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἓν διὰ δυοῖν.</foreign>"</quote><bibl>Allen.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"<foreign xml:lang="lat">Trahere animo</foreign> is always to revolve in the mind, not to let the thought of a thing escape from the mind."</quote><bibl>Kritzius.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"This verb is undoubtedly used in this passage for <foreign xml:lang="lat">decipere.</foreign> Compare Tibull. Eleg. iii. 6, 45: <foreign xml:lang="lat">Nec vos aut capiant pendentia brachia collo, Aut fallat blandâ sordida tingua piece.</foreign> Cic. Acad. iv. 20: <foreign xml:lang="lat">Sapientis vim maximam esse cavere, ne capiatur.</foreign>"</quote><bibl>Gerlach.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"At whom Marius directs this observation, it is impossible to tell. Gerlach, referring to Cic. Quæst. Acad. ii. 1, 2, thinks that Lucullus is meant. But if he supposes that Lucullus was present to the mind of Marius when he spoke, he is egregiously deceived, for Marius was forty years antecedent to Lucullus. It is possible, however, that Sallust, thinking of Lucullus when he wrote Marius's speech, may have fallen into an anachronism, and have attributed to Marius, whose character he had assumed, an observation which might justly have been made in his own day."</quote><bibl>Kritzius.</bibl></cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"That is, my lot, or condition, in which I was born, in which I had no hand in producing."</quote><bibl>Dietsch.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"Voltaire, Mohammed, Act. I., sec. iv., has expressed the sentiment of Sallust exactly: <quote xml:lang="fre"><l>Les mortels sont égaux, ce n'est point la naissance,</l> <l>C'est la seule vertu qui fait leur différence.</l></quote>"</quote><bibl>Burnouf.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"He would then ask the people whether they did not think that the ancestors of those men would have wished rather to leave a posterity like him, since they themselves had not risen to glory by their high birth, but by their virtue and heroic achievements?"</quote><bibl>Langhorne.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><bibl>Juvenal, viii. 138:</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat"><l>Incipit ipsorum contra te stare parentum</l> <l>Nobilitas, claramque facem præferre pudendis.</l></quote></cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"It was a common form of strong asseveration."</quote><bibl>Gerlach</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"A <foreign xml:lang="lat">hasta pura,</foreign> that is a spear without iron, was anciently the reward of a soldier the first time that he conquered in battle, Serv. ad Virg. Æn. vi. 760; it was afterward given to one who had struck down an enemy in a sally or skirmish, Lips. ad Polyb. de Milit. Rom. v. 17."</quote><bibl>Bernouf.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"Standards were also military rewards. Vopiscus relates that ten <foreign xml:lang="lat">hastœ purœ,</foreign> and four standards of two colors, were presented to Aurelian. Suetonius (Aug. 25) says that Agrippa was presented by Augustus, after his naval victory, with a standard of the color of the sea. These standards therefore, were not, as Badius Ascensius thinks, always taken from the enemy; though this was sometimes the case, as appears from <cit><bibl>Sil. Ital. xv. 261:</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat"><l>Tunc hasta viris, tunc martia cuique</l> <l>Vexilla, ut meritum, et prædæ libamina, dantur.</l></quote></cit>"</quote><bibl>Bernouf.</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><bibl>Sil. Ital. xv. 261:</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat"><l>Tunc hasta viris, tunc martia cuique</l> <l>Vexilla, ut meritum, et prædæ libamina, dantur.</l></quote></cit>"
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><bibl>Sil. Ital. xv. 255:</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat"><l>Phaleris hic pectora fulget:</l> <l>Hic torque aurato circumdat bellica colla.</l></quote></cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><bibl>Juvenal, xv. 60:</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">Ut læti phaleris omnes et torquibus omnes.</quote></cit> These passages show that

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"This speech, differing from the other speeches in Sallust both in words and thoughts, conveys a clear notion of that fierce and objurgatory eloquence which was natural to the rude manners and bold character of Marius. The phraseology is of an antique cast, and some of the words coarse. * * * But it is animated and fervid, rushing on like a torrent; and by language of such a character and structure, the nature and manners of Marius are excellently represented."</quote><bibl>Gerlach</bibl></cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"><quote>"<foreign xml:lang="lat">Inter,</foreign> for which Muller, from a conjecture of Glareanus, substituted <foreign xml:lang="lat">intra,</foreign> is supported by all the manuscripts, and ought not to be altered, although <foreign xml:lang="lat">intra</foreign> would have been more exact, as the signification of <foreign xml:lang="lat">inter</foreign> is of greater extent, and includes that of <foreign xml:lang="lat">intra. CAPITULO CVI: De uesica Vesica autem uariis et interdum acutis, interdum longis morbis obnoxia est. Communis omnium est urinae difficultas. Discrimina tamen in hac ipsa non sunt mediocria. Vnum genus est quo subinde urinae desiderium est, sed paulum eius emittitur. Totius tamen diei noctisque spatio plus quam sat est redditur. Id cum inueterauerit, tabe quadam hominem consumit. Huic accedit ipsius naturalis dolor maximeque urina et incipiente desinente. Nonnumquam uero idem dolor etiam uentrem imum coxasque etiam latera complectitur. Solent enim uesicae uitiis affici renes sicut illorum magis haec quoque afficitur.

Alius morbus est tormento crudelior, ubi urina uix et non nisi magna ui exprimitur. Sed hoc ipso cruciat quod urina continetur. Accedit calor quidam ruborque et circa pubem cum dolore tumor atque durities; nonnumquam uomitus. Interdum etiam tenuis urina in ipsa crura destillat. Saepe quod descendit purulentum est. Quae omnia quibusdam ulceribus natis oriri uidentur. Interdum autem aeque ulceribus ceterisque isdem obstantibus, urina tenuis est et aut nigra aut pallida aut cruenta simulque quaedam concreta prorumpunt.

Ac si scabies uesicam occupauit, multa et mali odoris urina est, eaque bullat et quaedam muccis furfuribusue similia ostendit. Subestque ut in prioribus, iunctarum quoque partium dolor simulque naturalis ipsius maxime dum urina descendit.

Cod. Tol. 7-12 Emerita

CAPITULO CVII: De calculis in uesica Praeter haec, calculi quoque in uesica nascuntur, urinae difficultate et magno dolore aeque accedentibus. Rursus ubi etiam sine dolore praeter modum urina descendit, contrario modo tabes hominem consumit. Atque ex ea quoque modo tenuis profluit, modo turbida et crassa. Et haec quidem fere in hac parte incidere consuerunt.

Commune uero ad urinae difficultatem remedium est his cibis uti: qui boni suci sunt, acria salsa, uinum uetus austerum uitare, leni ambulatione uti. Item leni frictione citra sudorem.

Rursus alia diuersorum malorum diuersa remedia sunt. Ergo quibus et multa et nimis crebra inter dolores urina destillat, eos oportet in balneum ire. Ibi multa aqua calida fouere inguina, calida omnia adsumere, deinde uomere. Postero die ratio est lauari eadem, deinde dimidio minus quam consuetudine cibi capere atque inuicem altero die hoc, altero, illo modo se curare. Si longius malum factum est, fouere inferiores cotidie partes aqua calida in qua lini semen decoctum sit, in eadem residere umbilico tenus. Ceratum ex oleo uetere facere eoque pubem proximasque partes circumdare. Abstinere ab omnibus quibus urinae mouendae sunt, potionem quoque quam parcissime adsumere, cibos capere ex media materia uel etiam imbellicissima, saepe sorbitiones quoque, at nihil frigidi neque edere neque bibere. Vomitus interponere, aluum, sialiter non respondet, uel ducere uel aliquo medicamento mouere. Si uero ulcera uel scabies uesicam occupauit, lacte uenter soluendus est. Demittendus homo in aquam calidam oleo mixtam, potui danda aqua quae comprimat. Mel quoque et dulcia omnia uidentur utilia. Imponendum super ceratum est ex irino uel cyprino factum.

Longe periculosissima in calculis res est. Calculi modo integra, modo scabra uesica nascuntur. Integra nati aut semper in ea mouentur—quos ab innatando πλωτοῦσ Graeci uocant—, aut in ipsa uesicae ceruice orti ubi primo, conquiescunt. Postea, magnitudine adsumpta, pondere in uesicam prolabuntur. Hi uero qui ulceribus innati sunt, primum his quasi crestae inhaerent; deinde, quia innatuerunt, non continentur; inde quoque pondere decidunt. Vtrique ex immobilibus mobiles fiunt. Solutique calculi qui prius ulceri inhaeserat, signum est contra morem sanguis profusus et eo plus quo is asperior est. Carunculaeque tenues subalbidae cartilagini similes secutae in quibusdam etiam plenior. Omnis autem calculus qui uel a principio uagus natus uel postea factus est modo uesicae ceruicem urget, modo ab ea recedit. Initium est eo delapsi, dolor auctus et urina difficilior. Ac nisi inde retro redit et tortus exstet, febriculas excitat.

Igitur ubi prima esse nota calculi coepit, experienda sunt remedia quae nonnumquam citra scalpelli curationem, si maxime coneris, is uitium discutiunt ieiunoque cotidie paulum quod deuoret resinae terebinthinae dandum est. Id enim et calculos discutit et quaedam arenosa coeuntia.

Cod.Tol. 7-12 Emerita

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <bibl>Non. p. 342M</bibl> <quote>Statu esse optimo constitutam rem publicam, quae ex tribus generibus illis, <pb n="p.320"/> regali et optumati et populari, confusa modice nec puniendo inritet animum inmanem ac ferum. </quote> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <bibl>Non. 263M</bibl> <quote>ut Carneadi respondeatis, qui saepe optimas causas ingenii calumnia ludificari solet. <pb n="p.335"/> <pb n="p.336"/> </quote> </cit>

Non. 125M,318M,534M

Nam cum quaereretur ex eo, quo scelere inpulsus mare haberet infestum uno myoparone, 'Eodem', inquit, 'quo tu orbem terrae.'

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <bibl>Lactant. Div. Inst. 5.12.5</bibl> <quote>Quaero, si duo sint, quorum alter optimus vir, aequissimus, summa iustitia, singulari fide, alter insigni scelere et audacia, et si in eo sit errore civitas, ut bonum illum virum sceleratum, facinorosum, nefarium putet, contra autem, <pb n="p.342"/> qui sit improbissimus, existimet esse summa probitate ac fide, proque hac opinione omnium civium bonus ille vir vexetur, rapiatur, manus ei denique adferantur, effodiantur oculi, damnetur, vinciatur, uratur, exterminetur egeat,</quote> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <bibl>Gel. N.A. 1.22.8</bibl> <quote>Non gravarer, Laeli, nisi et hos velle putarem et ipse cuperem te quoque aliquam partem huius nostri sermonis attingere, praesertim cum heri <pb n="p.344"/> ipse dixeris te nobis etiam superfuturum. Verum id quidem fieri non potest; ne desis, omnes te rogamus. </quote> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <bibl>Non. 323M, 324M</bibl> <quote>sed iuventuti nostrae minime audiendus; quippe, si ita sensit, ut loquitur, est homo impurus; sin aliter, quod malo, oratio est tamen immanis. </quote> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <bibl>Lactant. Div. Inst. 6.8.6</bibl> <quote>Est quidem vera lex recta ratio naturae congruens, diffusa in omnes, constans, sempiterna, quae vocet ad officium iubendo, vetando a fraude deterreat; quae tamen neque probos frustra iubet aut vetat nec improbos iubendo aut vetando movet. Huic legi nec obrogari fas est neque derogari ex hac aliquid licet neque tota abrogari potest, nec vero aut per senatum aut per populum solvi hac lege possumus, neque est quaerendus explanator aut interpres eius alius, nec erit alia lex Romae, alia Athenis, alia nunc, alia posthac, sed et omnes gentes et omni tempore una lex et sempiterna et immutabilis continebit, unusque erit communis quasi magister et imperator omnium deus, ille legis huius inventor, disceptator, lator; cui qui non parebit, ipse se fugiet ac naturam hominis aspernatus hoc ipso luet maximas poenas, etiamsi cetera supplicia, quae putantur, effugerit. </quote> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <bibl>August. C.D. 22.6</bibl> <quote>nullum bellum suscipi a civitate optima nisi aut pro fide aut pro salute.</quote> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <bibl>Isid. Orig. 18.1.2sq.</bibl> <quote>Illa iniusta bella sunt, quae sunt sine causa suscepta. Nam extra ulciscendi aut propulsandorum hostium causam bellum geri iustum nullum potest.</quote> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <bibl>Isid. Orig. 18.1.2sq.</bibl> <quote>Nullum bellum iustum habetur nisi denuntiatum, nisi indictum, nisi repetitis rebus. </quote> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <bibl>Non. 498M</bibl> <quote>Noster autem populus sociis defendendis terrarum iam omnium potitus est. <pb n="p.346"/> </quote> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <bibl>August. C.D. 19.21</bibl> <quote>Cur igitur deus homini, animus imperat corpori, ratio libidini ceterisque vitiosis animi partibus? </quote> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <bibl>August. c. Iul. 4.12.61</bibl> <quote>An non cernimus optimo cuique dominatum ab ipsa natura cum summa utilitate infirmorum datum? Cur igitur deus homini, animus imperat corpori, ratio libidini iracundiaeque et ceteris vitiosis eiusdem animi partibus?</quote> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <bibl>August. c. Iul. 4.12.61</bibl> <quote>Sed et imperandi et serviendi sunt dissimilitudines cognoscendae. Nam ut animus corpori dicitur imperare, dicitur etiam libidini, sed corpori ut rex civibus suis aut parens liberis, libidini autem ut servis dominus, quod eam coercet et frangit, sic regum, sic imperatorum, sic magistratuum, sic patrum, sic populorum imperia civibus sociisque praesunt ut corporibus animus, domini autem servos ita fatigant, ut optima pars animi, id est sapientia, eiusdem animi vitiosas imbecillasque partes, ut libidines, ut iracundias, ut perturbationes ceteras. </quote> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <bibl>August. C.D. 14.23</bibl> <quote>ut filiis imperari corporis membris propter oboediendi facilitatem, vitiosas vero animi partes ut servos asperiore imperio coerceri? </quote> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <bibl>Non. 109M</bibl> <quote>Est enim genus iniustae servitutis, cum ii sunt alterius, qui sui possunt esse; cum autem ii famulantur <gap reason="omitted"/> <pb n="p.347"/> </quote> </cit>
<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <bibl>Prisc. GL 2.399K</bibl> <quote>in quibus adsentior sollicitam et periculosam iustitiam non esse sapientis. </quote> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <bibl>Lactant. Div. Inst. 5.18.4</bibl> <quote>Vult plane virtus honorem, nec est virtutis ulla alia merces<gap reason="omitted"/> . Quam tamen illa accipit facile, exigit non acerbe<gap reason="omitted"/> . Huic tu viro quas divitias obicies? quae imperia? quae regna? qui ista putat humana, sua bona divina iudicat<gap reason="omitted"/> . Sed si aut ingrati universi aut <pb n="p.348"/> invidi multi aut inimici potentes suis virtutem praemiis spoliant, ne illa se multis solaciis oblectat maximeque suo decore se ipsa sustentat. </quote> </cit>

<cit xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0"> <bibl>August. C.D. 22.4</bibl> <quote>Quorum non corpora sunt in caelum elata; neque enim natura pateretur, ut id, quod esset e terra, nisi in terra maneret. </quote> </cit>
